---
title: "Exercise: Solving linear equation systems by hand"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

After this exercise you will be able to:

- Translate a small biology mixture problem into a system of linear equations
  and write it as $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$.
- Solve $2\times 2$ and $3\times 3$ systems **by hand** using substitution and
  Gaussian elimination with an augmented matrix.
- Check a solution by substituting it back into the original equations.
- Reproduce your by-hand answers in R with [`solve`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/solve.html).

This is the by-hand fluency you need for the written exam, so do the pen-and-
paper parts *before* you touch R.

## Background

A **bulk** sample is a mixture of cell types. If we know, for each cell type,
how much signal it contributes to each measured gene (its *signature*), then
the total measured signal is a weighted sum of the unknown cell-type amounts.
With as many genes measured as cell types present, recovering the amounts is
exactly solving a square linear system.

In standard form,
$$
A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b},
$$
where the column $j$ of $A$ is the signature of cell type $j$, the entry
$x_j$ is the unknown amount of cell type $j$, and $\mathbf{b}$ holds the bulk
measurements. Row $i$ of the system reads
$$
A_{i1}x_1 + A_{i2}x_2 + \dots + A_{in}x_n = b_i,
$$
the total signal for gene $i$.

Two solution methods:

- **Substitution** — solve one equation for one unknown and substitute into
  the others. Good for $2\times 2$.
- **Gaussian elimination** — use row operations (multiply a row by a non-zero
  number; add a multiple of one row to another) to reach **upper-triangular**
  form, then **back-substitute** from the bottom row up. Scales to any size.

## By hand

Do these on paper. Show the row operations, not just the final numbers.

### Problem 1 — a 2×2 mixture (substitution)

A blood sample is a mix of two cell types. Per unit, their signatures for
genes G1 and G2 are:

| | Type A | Type B |
|--|:--:|:--:|
| G1 | 3 | 1 |
| G2 | 1 | 2 |

The bulk measurements are G1 $= 11$ and G2 $= 7$.

(a) Write the system as two equations and as $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$.
(b) Solve for the amounts $x_1$ (Type A) and $x_2$ (Type B) by **substitution**.
(c) **Check** your answer in both original equations.

### Problem 2 — the same 2×2 by Gaussian elimination

Re-solve Problem 1 using **Gaussian elimination on the augmented matrix**
$[\,A \mid \mathbf{b}\,]$. Reach upper-triangular form, then back-substitute.
You should of course get the same answer as in Problem 1.

### Problem 3 — a 3×3 mixture (Gaussian elimination)

Three cell types, three marker genes give the system
$$
\begin{aligned}
1x_1 + 2x_2 + 1x_3 &= 8\\
2x_1 + 1x_2 + 1x_3 &= 7\\
1x_1 + 1x_2 + 3x_3 &= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

(a) Write the augmented matrix $[\,A \mid \mathbf{b}\,]$.
(b) Eliminate column 1, then column 2, to reach upper-triangular form.
(c) Back-substitute to find $x_1, x_2, x_3$, and **check** all three equations.

## In R

Use R only to **verify** the answers you already found by hand.

```r
# Problem 1 / 2 — the 2x2 system
A <- matrix(c(3, 1,
              1, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(11, 7)

x <- solve(A, b)   # solve A x = b
x
# expected: your Type A and Type B amounts from the by-hand answer

# Check: does A %*% x reproduce b?
A %*% x
```

```r
# Problem 3 — the 3x3 system
A3 <- matrix(c(1, 2, 1,
               2, 1, 1,
               1, 1, 3), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
b3 <- c(8, 7, 10)

x3 <- solve(A3, b3)
x3

# Verify the residual is (numerically) zero
A3 %*% x3 - b3
```

Note `byrow = TRUE`: it fills the matrix row by row, so the R code reads in the
same order as the equations on paper. (The default, `byrow = FALSE`, fills
column by column — a common source of confusion.)

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — setting up A and b"}
Each **equation** is one **row**. The numbers multiplying $x_1, x_2, \dots$ go
into $A$ in order; the right-hand number goes into $\mathbf{b}$. For Problem 1,
the two equations are $3x_1 + 1x_2 = 11$ and $1x_1 + 2x_2 = 7$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — substitution"}
From the second equation of Problem 1, $x_1 = 7 - 2x_2$. Substitute that into
the first equation to get a single equation in $x_2$ alone, solve it, then
back-substitute to get $x_1$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — choosing the elimination multiplier"}
To kill the entry in row $i$, column $j$ using a pivot row $p$ (which has a
non-zero entry in column $j$), use
$$
\text{R}_i \;\leftarrow\; \text{R}_i - \frac{A_{ij}}{A_{pj}}\,\text{R}_p .
$$
Apply the operation to the **whole** augmented row, including the $\mathbf{b}$
entry.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 4 — back-substitution"}
After reaching upper-triangular form, the **last** row has only one unknown —
solve it. Carry that value into the row above, which now also has one remaining
unknown, and continue upward.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 5 — the R functions"}
[`matrix`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matrix.html) builds a matrix (use
`byrow = TRUE`); [`solve`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/solve.html) with two arguments
solves $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$;
[`%*%`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matmult.html) is matrix multiplication, used here
to check the answer.
:::

## Solutions

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}

### Problem 1 — substitution

(a) Equations: $3x_1 + x_2 = 11$ and $x_1 + 2x_2 = 7$. In standard form,
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 11 \\ 7 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

(b) From equation 2, $x_1 = 7 - 2x_2$. Substitute into equation 1:
$$
3(7 - 2x_2) + x_2 = 11 \;\Rightarrow\; 21 - 6x_2 + x_2 = 11
\;\Rightarrow\; -5x_2 = -10 \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = 2.
$$
Then $x_1 = 7 - 2(2) = 3$. So **$x_1 = 3$ (Type A), $x_2 = 2$ (Type B)**.

(c) Check: $3(3) + 1(2) = 11\ \checkmark$ and $1(3) + 2(2) = 7\ \checkmark$.

### Problem 2 — Gaussian elimination

$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 3 & 1 & 11 \\ 1 & 2 & 7 \end{array}\right]
\xrightarrow{\;\text{R2}-\frac13\text{R1}\;}
\left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 3 & 1 & 11 \\ 0 & \tfrac{5}{3} & \tfrac{10}{3} \end{array}\right]
$$
Bottom row: $\tfrac{5}{3}x_2 = \tfrac{10}{3} \Rightarrow x_2 = 2$.
Back-substitute into R1: $3x_1 + 2 = 11 \Rightarrow x_1 = 3$. Same answer.

### Problem 3 — 3×3 Gaussian elimination

(a) Augmented matrix:
$$
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 8\\
2 & 1 & 1 & 7\\
1 & 1 & 3 & 10
\end{array}\right]
$$

(b) Clear column 1 with $\text{R2}-2\text{R1}$ and $\text{R3}-\text{R1}$:
$$
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 8\\
0 & -3 & -1 & -9\\
0 & -1 & 2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
$$
Clear column 2 with $\text{R3} - \tfrac{1}{3}\text{R2}$
(since $-1 = \tfrac{1}{3}\cdot(-3)$):
$$
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & 2 & 1 & 8\\
0 & -3 & -1 & -9\\
0 & 0 & \tfrac{7}{3} & 5
\end{array}\right]
$$
(Row 3: $2 - \tfrac13(-1) = \tfrac{7}{3}$ and $2 - \tfrac13(-9) = 5$.)

(c) Back-substitute:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tfrac{7}{3}x_3 &= 5 &&\Rightarrow\; x_3 = \tfrac{15}{7}\approx 2.143\\
-3x_2 - x_3 &= -9 &&\Rightarrow\; -3x_2 = -9 + \tfrac{15}{7} = -\tfrac{48}{7}
   \;\Rightarrow\; x_2 = \tfrac{16}{7}\approx 2.286\\
x_1 + 2x_2 + x_3 &= 8 &&\Rightarrow\; x_1 = 8 - \tfrac{32}{7} - \tfrac{15}{7}
   = 8 - \tfrac{47}{7} = \tfrac{9}{7}\approx 1.286
\end{aligned}
$$
So $x_1 = \tfrac{9}{7},\ x_2 = \tfrac{16}{7},\ x_3 = \tfrac{15}{7}$.

Check equation 1: $\tfrac{9}{7} + 2\cdot\tfrac{16}{7} + \tfrac{15}{7}
= \tfrac{9 + 32 + 15}{7} = \tfrac{56}{7} = 8\ \checkmark$.
(Equations 2 and 3 check out the same way.)

### R verification

```r
A <- matrix(c(3, 1, 1, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(11, 7)
solve(A, b)
# [1] 3 2

A3 <- matrix(c(1, 2, 1,
               2, 1, 1,
               1, 1, 3), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
b3 <- c(8, 7, 10)
solve(A3, b3)
# [1] 1.285714 2.285714 2.142857   (= 9/7, 16/7, 15/7)

A3 %*% solve(A3, b3) - b3
# all entries ~0 (tiny floating-point residual)
```

:::

## Where this leads

Every system here had **exactly one** solution. That is not guaranteed:
**Day 2** builds these systems in R, and asks what happens when $A$ is
*singular* or *non-square* — the cases of **no solution** and **infinitely
many solutions**, which correspond to a deconvolution problem being
over- or under-determined.
